title: The Sunday Wind-Up Ritual
subtitle: A ten-minute Sunday evening ritual for closing the week cleanly
excerpt: A ten-minute Sunday evening ritual for closing the week before the next one begins. The ritual has three steps: brain dump, tomorrow's anchor, and a physical signal that the week is done. Reduces Sunday-night dread.

practice.whenToUse: Use on Sunday evenings when the coming week is already making noise before it starts. Good for reducing the background hum of undone tasks during what should be rest time.

content.text: A ten-minute ritual for closing the week on Sunday evening. The Sunday-night dread comes partly from tasks and concerns floating in working memory without a container. This ritual gives them one, briefly, so they can be put down.
New to rituals as a practice? Read

Write down everything that is currently in your head about the coming week. Tasks, concerns, things you have not decided yet, things you are worried about forgetting. Write without organising. The point is to get it out of working memory and onto paper, not to create a system.

From the brain dump, identify one thing that, if you do it tomorrow, will make Monday feel like it moved. One thing. Write it on a separate line or a sticky note. That is your anchor for tomorrow morning.

Choose a physical act that signals the week is done: close the notebook, put the list face-down, turn off the desk lamp, move to a different room. The act matters less than its consistency. Do the same thing each Sunday so the nervous system learns it as a signal.
After the close, the week is done. If a task surfaces in your mind before Monday, note it on your phone and put it back down. It is in the container. It can wait.
